title: Extending mixtime
text: Time units building blocks calendars. Time units S7 classes inherit mt_unit, purely abstract time unit class. practice, time units inherit either: mt_tz_unit civil-time units whose boundaries depend timezone (e.g. months, days, hours, …), mt_loc_unit astronomical-time units whose boundaries depend geographic location (e.g. solar days). time units accept numeric .data argument representing size unit (e.g. 1L “one month”). Additional arguments needed time unit can defined S7 : inheriting properties mt_tz_unit (tz IANA names) mt_loc_unit (lon, lat, alt), defining custom S7 properties S7::new_class(properties = ...). name time unit’s S7 class becomes internal identifier unit, unique across calendars. class name important users calendar, descriptive developers. time units typically exported within calendar object (shown ) accessed via $ notation.
code: # Timezone-aware Symmetry454 year and month units S7::new_class("tu_symmetry454_year",  parent = mt_tz_unit) #> <tu_symmetry454_year> class #> @ parent     : <mixtime::mt_tz_unit> #> @ constructor: function(.data, tz) {...} #> @ validator  : <NULL> #> @ properties : #>  $ tz: <character> S7::new_class("tu_symmetry454_month", parent = mt_tz_unit) #> <tu_symmetry454_month> class #> @ parent     : <mixtime::mt_tz_unit> #> @ constructor: function(.data, tz) {...} #> @ validator  : <NULL> #> @ properties : #>  $ tz: <character>

text: calendar named collection time units, created new_calendar(). optional inherit argument pulls units existing calendar (units defined ... take precedence). useful inherit base calendar defines common time units civil astronomical calendars. base calendars provided mixtime : cal_time_civil_midnight: civil time units anchored midnight (e.g. days, hours, minutes, seconds, …) cal_time_solar_***: solar time units anchored sunrise, noon, sunset midnight (e.g. solar days) cal_time_lunar: lunar time units (e.g. lunar months, lunar phases) case, time unit weeks exactly matches ISO week unit, can reuse existing cal_isoweek$week unit rather defining new one. implementation Symmetry454 calendar week structure ISO calendar (epoch, 7-day length, Monday start). calendar unit shares name different structure (e.g. month comprised 4 5 weeks), new time unit unique S7 class must defined (similar year month units ). Unit constructors now accessible via $: time units can used chronons/cycles within mixtime() (also linear_time(), cyclical_time()). temporal relationships units must defined calendar arithmetic methods described .
code: cal_symmetry454 <- new_calendar(   year  = S7::new_class("tu_symmetry454_year",  parent = mt_tz_unit),   month = S7::new_class("tu_symmetry454_month", parent = mt_tz_unit),   week  = cal_isoweek$week,   # Inherit civil-time units (day, hour, minute, second, ...)   inherit = cal_time_civil_midnight,   class = "cal_symmetry454" )  cal_symmetry454 #> <cal_symmetry454> #> Time units: #>   - year #>   - month #>   - week #>   - day #>   - ampm #>   - hour #>   - minute #>   - second #>   - millisecond cal_symmetry454$year(1L) #> <tu_symmetry454_year> int 1 #>  @ tz: chr "" cal_symmetry454$month(1L, tz = "UTC") #> <tu_symmetry454_month> int 1 #>  @ tz: chr "UTC"

text: Two S7 generics drive calendar calculations: chronon_cardinality() chronon_divmod(). functions used manipulate time, converting units, generating sequences, comparing time points. details work important users calendar, main extension points developers creating new calendars. time unit useful mixtime, minimum chronon_cardinality() method next coarser unit must defined. Time units irregular relationships (e.g. days → months) also require chronon_divmod() efficiently compute relationships units. Defining methods time units different calendars also possible, allows interoperability calendars. cases, calendars share common base calendar (e.g. cal_time_civil_midnight) provides common time unit (e.g. civil days) methods converting different calendars (e.g. civil midnight solar midnight).

title: Extending mixtime
text: cardinality number finer units fit inside coarser unit. example, 7 days week, cardinality cal_isoweek$day(1L) terms cal_isoweek$week(1L) 7. units fixed relationship (e.g. 7 days per week) argument can ignored. variable disambiguates point time variable cardinalities (e.g. days per month), internal numeric representation time coarser unit (e.g. months since epoch days → months). Symmetry454 calendar every year exactly 12 months (fixed relationship). number weeks month follows repeating 4–5–4 pattern across quarter (months 1–3: 4, 5, 4 weeks; months 4–6: 4, 5, 4 weeks; ). circsum() helper computes many weeks fall n-month period summing appropriate slice cycle, especially useful month unit size greater 1. Symmetry454 uses 293-year leap-week cycle: year leap year (gaining 53rd week December) (52 * year + 146) %% 293 < 52. differs substantially familiar Gregorian leap-day rule produces 52 leap years every 293-year period. Cardinality methods required adjacent time units (e.g. month → year, week → month), can defined non-adjacent units optimization. direct cardinality method defined, mixtime attempt derive computing cardinality along path intermediate units. cardinality methods also define units finer/coarser operations requiring graph traversal, essential first unit finer unit second unit coarser unit. inverse cardinality method (units flipped) automatically derived, define one direction (finer → coarser) relationship.
code: chronon_cardinality(cal_isoweek$day(1L), cal_isoweek$week(1L)) #> [1] 7 chronon_cardinality(cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$month(1L), at = 0L) # Jan 1970 #> [1] 31 chronon_cardinality(cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$month(1L), at = 1L) # Feb 1970 #> [1] 28 chronon_cardinality(cal_gregorian$day(1L), cal_gregorian$month(1L), at = 25L) # Feb 1972 (leap year) #> [1] 29 # Each Symmetry454 year has 12 months S7::method(chronon_cardinality, list(cal_symmetry454$month, cal_symmetry454$year)) <-   function(x, y, at = NULL) {     vctrs::vec_data(y) * 12L / vctrs::vec_data(x)   } chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$month(1L), cal_symmetry454$year(1L)) #> [1] 12 S7::method(chronon_cardinality, list(cal_symmetry454$week, cal_symmetry454$month)) <-   function(x, y, at = NULL) {     # The number of weeks in each n-month period     month_size <- vctrs::vec_data(y)     nweeks_cyc <- circsum(c(4L, 5L, 4L), month_size)      # Find which n-month period we're in based on the "at" position (months since epoch)     period <- at %% length(nweeks_cyc) + 1L      nweeks <- nweeks_cyc[period]      # Add the extra week to December for Symmetry454 leap years.     # A year is a leap year when (52*year + 146) %% 293 < 52.     m1 <- at * month_size     contains_dec <- which((m1 %% 12L) >= (12L - month_size))     year <- 1970L + m1[contains_dec] %/% 12L     is_leap_year <- ((52 * year + 146L) %% 293L) < 52L      nweeks[contains_dec[is_leap_year]] <- nweeks[contains_dec[is_leap_year]] + 1L      # Scale by the number of weeks in the week time unit     nweeks / vctrs::vec_data(x)   }  # The 4-5-4 cycle across a full Symmetry454 year chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(1L), at = 0:11) #>  [1] 4 5 4 4 5 4 4 5 4 4 5 5  # 1970 is a leap year (53 weeks), so December has 5 weeks instead of 4 chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(1L), at = 11L) #> [1] 5  # Non-leap year: December has the usual 4 weeks chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(1L), at = 12:23) # Dec 1971 #>  [1] 4 5 4 4 5 4 4 5 4 4 5 4  # The number of weeks in a multi-month period is the sum of the weeks in each month chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(2L), at = 0:5) #> [1]  9  8  9  9  8 10 # The number of weeks in a Symmetry454 year (derived from week → month → year) # Note that the leap year in 1970 (at = 0) produces 53 weeks via week → month. chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cal_symmetry454$year(1L), at = 0:4) #> [1] 53 52 52 52 52  # The number of days in a Symmetry454 year (derived from day → week → month → year) chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$day(1L), cal_symmetry454$year(1L), at = 0:4) #> [1] 371 364 364 364 364 # The number of Symmetry454 years in a month is the inverse of months → years (1/12) chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$year(1L), cal_symmetry454$month(1L)) #> [1] 0.08333333  # The number of Symmetry454 months in 2 weeks (requires `at` since weeks → months is irregular) chronon_cardinality(cal_symmetry454$month(1L), cal_symmetry454$week(2L), at = 0:4) #> [1] 0.5 0.4 0.5 0.5 0.4

title: Extending mixtime
text: divmod operation combined division modulus converts time units defined chronon_divmod() methods. methods required efficiently convert units irregular relationships, days → months. regular relationships (e.g. days → weeks) divmod automatically derived chronon_cardinality() methods alone. chronon_divmod(, , x) converts times x units time points units. example, date “1970-02-15” day 45 since epoch (x = 45 = cal_gregorian$day(1L)), converted months (= cal_gregorian$month(1L)) gives 1 month 14 days (div = 1, mod = 14). Methods chronon_divmod() return list : div - quotient (chronons units) mod - remainder (leftover chronons) Note time values zero-indexed, $div = 0 January 1970, $mod = 0 first day month. result can interpreted $div = 1 February 1970 $mod = 14 14 remaining days (.e. 15th). Symmetry454 calendar irregular cardinality weeks → months (4–5–4 pattern leap weeks), divmod method required. key challenge correctly accounting 293-year leap year pattern. Symmetry454 leap weeks follow structured sub-cycle pattern: 293 years decompose five groups (45+79+45+79+45 years), decomposes primary 17-year sub-cycles (3 leap years) secondary 11-year sub-cycles (2 leap years). majority following code uses structure efficiently count many leap weeks occurred given week number cycle. general strategy used code first remove complexity 293-year leap week cycle regularising week count fixed 52 weeks per year (essentially subtracting cumulative leap weeks cycle). regularised week count easier work 4-5-4 pattern, use find converted month (div) number complete n-month cycles plus number complete n-months within current cycle, regularised week remainder (mod) remaining weeks within current n-month period. final step adjust remainder account leap weeks removed regularisation. complication relates leap week pattern, chronon_divmod() methods complicated generality converting n-unit time granules (e.g. converting 1 week 2 month chronons). generality needed, method can raise error time unit size 1 (e.g. (vctrs::vec_data() != 1L) stop("...")). inverse relationship must also defined convert months → weeks. converting coarser → finer units, integer values x introduce temporal indeterminacy. convention divmod methods left-align conversion (use first moment month). brevity, inverse divmod method shown can found package’s cal_sym454 source code direct chronon_divmod() method defined pair units, mixtime attempt derive chaining together divmod operations along shortest path intermediate units.

Account for leap weeks by regularising x to have a fixed 52 weeks per year      # The symmetrical sub-cycles of the 293-year leap week cycle are:     #   17+11+17 + 17+17+11+17+17 + 17+11+17 + 17+17+11+17+17 + 17+11+17     #   = 45 + 79 + 45 + 79 + 45 = 293     # Primary (length 17) sub-cycles have 3 leap years: 00100000100000100     # Secondary (length 11) sub-cycles have 2 leap years: 00100000100     leaps_cycle_17 <- function(w) (w >= 157L) + (w >= 470L) + (w >= 783L)     leaps_cycle_11 <- function(w) (w >= 157L) + (w >= 470L)     leaps_cycle_45 <- function(w) {       ifelse(w < 887L,  leaps_cycle_17(w),       ifelse(w < 1461L, 3L + leaps_cycle_11(w - 887L),                         5L + leaps_cycle_17(w - 1461L)))     }     leaps_cycle_79 <- function(w) {       ifelse(w < 887L,  leaps_cycle_17(w),       ifelse(w < 1774L, 3L  + leaps_cycle_17(w - 887L),       ifelse(w < 2348L, 6L  + leaps_cycle_11(w - 1774L),       ifelse(w < 3235L, 8L  + leaps_cycle_17(w - 2348L),                         11L + leaps_cycle_17(w - 3235L)))))     }     leaps_symmetry454 <- function(x) {       # There are 15288 weeks in a full 293-year cycle (293*52 + 52 leap weeks)       w <- x %% 15288L       x %/% 15288L * 52L +       ifelse(w < 2348L,  leaps_cycle_45(w),       ifelse(w < 6470L,  8L  + leaps_cycle_79(w - 2348L),       ifelse(w < 8818L,  22L + leaps_cycle_45(w - 6470L),       ifelse(w < 12940L, 30L + leaps_cycle_79(w - 8818L),                          44L + leaps_cycle_45(w - 12940L)))))     }      # Offset x to align with the nearest 293-year cycle boundary before the epoch.     # There are 349 leap years between year 1-W1 and the 1970-W1 epoch, and the     # nearest cycle start is (1969*52 + 349) %% (293*52 + 52) = 11009 weeks before epoch.     x_cyc <- x + 11009L + week_size  # right align multi-week units     n_leaps <- leaps_symmetry454(x_cyc)      # Regularise x to have exactly 52 weeks per year by subtracting leap weeks.     # (37 leap years occur in the cycle before the epoch, so we add 37 back.)     x_reg <- x - n_leaps + 37L      # 2. Use the 4-5-4 pattern to find the month (div) and week remainder (mod)     ## The number of weeks in each n-month period     month_size <- vctrs::vec_data(to)     weeks_len <- circsum(c(4L, 5L, 4L), month_size)      ## The total weeks in a full n-month cycle     weeks_tot <- sum(weeks_len)      ## Find which n-month cycle we're in based on the regularised week count     period_full <- x_reg %/% weeks_tot      ## Find which part within the n-month cycle we're in     weeks_seq  <- cumsum(weeks_len[-length(weeks_len)])     period_part <- rowSums(outer(x_reg %% weeks_tot, weeks_seq, ">="))      # div: total complete n-month cycles + complete n-months within the current cycle     div <- period_full * length(weeks_len) + period_part     # mod: remaining (regularised) weeks within the current n-month period     mod <- x_reg %% weeks_tot - c(0L, weeks_seq)[period_part + 1L]      # 3. Adjust the remainder to account for leap weeks that were removed during regularisation.      # Identify leap weeks re-using cumulative in-cycle leap week counts: leaps_symmetry454()     # If the week added a leap week from the previous, then it itself must be a leap week.     # Applied only to regularised 52/53rd weeks of the year (only these weeks can be leap weeks)     last_weeks  <- which(x_reg %% 52L >= 51L)     leap_weeks  <- last_weeks[n_leaps[last_weeks] - leaps_symmetry454(x_cyc[last_weeks] - week_size) > 0L]     mod[leap_weeks] <- mod[leap_weeks] + 1L  # restore leap week to remainder      # Scale mod back to the original n-unit week size     mod <- mod %/% week_size      # Return the divmod result     list(div = div, mod = mod)   } # Week 19 (0-indexed) of 1970 is the 2nd week (div=1) of May 1970 (mod=4) with(cal_symmetry454, chronon_divmod(week(1L), month(1L), 18L)) #> $div #> [1] 4 #>  #> $mod #> [1] 1  # Week 52 (0-indexed) is the leap week of 1970; it is the 5th week (div=4) of Dec 1970 (mod=11) with(cal_symmetry454, chronon_divmod(week(1L), month(1L), 52L)) #> $div #> [1] 11 #>  #> $mod #> [1] 4 # The 5th fortnight of 1970 is the 3rd fortnight (div=2) of Feb 1970 (mod=1) with(cal_symmetry454, chronon_divmod(week(2L), month(1L), 4L)) #> $div #> [1] 1 #>  #> $mod #> [1] 2 # Divmod for converting days → years is derived from days → weeks → months → years # Gregorian day 839 since unix epoch (1972-04-19) is symmetry454 day 101 (mod=100) of year 1972 (div=2) with(cal_symmetry454, chronon_divmod(day(1L), year(1L), 839L)) #> $div #> [1] 2 #>  #> $mod #> [1] 100

title: Extending mixtime
text: time unit two format methods: time_unit_full() returns singular full name unit (e.g. “Symmetry454 year”) time_unit_abbr() returns short abbreviation unit (e.g. “Y”) full name used messages (e.g. “Can’t convert Symmetry454 year day”) abbreviation used displaying time intervals durations. time unit abbreviations also used default time formatting string, can now create view Symmetry454 linear time vector: linear time helper functions can also used new calendar: Cyclical time vectors can also created new calendar, default formatting string use time unit abbreviations: Cyclical time labels default 0-indexing, month year labelled “10” November. can improved defining custom label methods described next section.
code: S7::method(time_unit_full, cal_symmetry454$year)  <- function(x) "Symmetry454 year" S7::method(time_unit_abbr, cal_symmetry454$year)  <- function(x) "Y" S7::method(time_unit_full, cal_symmetry454$month) <- function(x) "Symmetry454 month" S7::method(time_unit_abbr, cal_symmetry454$month) <- function(x) "M" linear_time(as.Date("1955-11-12"), chronon = cal_symmetry454$year(1L)) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Y1955 year(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Y1955 # Week of the month cyclical_time(as.Date("1955-11-12"), chronon = week(1L), cycle = month(1L), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] W02  # Month of the year month_of_year(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] M10

title: Extending mixtime
text: time label functions describe granule time (e.g. years, months, weeks) displayed. two types labels consider: linear time labels continuous time units (usually coarsest time unit, years), cyclical time labels time units nest (cycle ) another granule (e.g. months within year, days within week). linear_labels() cyclical_labels() methods return character vectors length input time indices . enforced, recommend using following argument names common label options: label: TRUE, return labelled values (e.g. “January”, “February”, …), otherwise return unlabelled values (e.g. “1”, “2”, …) abbreviate: TRUE (label TRUE), return abbreviated labels (e.g. “Jan”, “Feb”, …), otherwise return full labels (e.g. “January”, “February”, …)

title: Extending mixtime
text: cases, default linear labels provided linear_labels() sufficient, simply return internal numeric representation time point (e.g. “0”, “1”, “2”, …). example default improved correctly labelling years era boundary (e.g. “2BC”, “1BC”, “1”, “2”, … instead “-1”, “0”, “1”, “2”, …). transition 1 BC 1 AD appropriately labelled: ’ll remove “Y” prefix year format strings time format methods make labels readable.
code: S7::method(linear_labels, cal_symmetry454$year) <- function(granule, i, ...) {   ifelse(i <= 0L, paste0(-i + 1L, "BC"), i) } year(-1:2, calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[4]> #> [1] Y2BC Y1BC Y1   Y2

title: Extending mixtime
text: common need custom labels cyclical time units, since cyclical time points often familiar labels (e.g. Jan, Feb, … months years) 1-indexed (e.g. 1, 2, … days month). default method cyclical_labels() simply uses internal 0-indexed numeric representation time point. cyclical labels (e.g. day week) inherited cal_isoweek since reused ISO week unit, correctly labelled days week (e.g. “Mon”, “Tue”, …). linear cyclical label methods place, formatting now produces correctly indexed informative labels Symmetry454 time vectors: default formats can improved better suit Symmetry454 calendar. example, week format YYYY WW inherited cal_isoweek YYYY-MM-WW informative Symmetry454 since weeks nested within months. default format months MM, YYYY-MM informative. time format methods allow us change default formats linear cyclical time vectors.
code: # Labels for months of the year, essentially the same as Gregorian months in years. S7::method(cyclical_labels, list(cal_symmetry454$month, cal_symmetry454$year)) <-   function(granule, cycle, i, label = FALSE, abbreviate = FALSE, ...) {     if (label) {       # Index into R's localised month name objects (month.name and month.abb)       if (abbreviate) month.abb[i + 1L] else month.name[i + 1L]     } else {       # Use i + 1L for 1-indexing months (so January is 1, February is 2, ...)       sprintf("%02d", i + 1L)     }   }  # Labels for weeks of the month are simply 1-indexed (e.g. "W1", "W2", ...) S7::method(cyclical_labels, list(cal_symmetry454$week, cal_symmetry454$month)) <-   function(granule, cycle, i, ...) {     as.character(i + 1L)   } # Month of year month_of_year(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] M11 # Week of month cyclical_time(as.Date("1955-11-12"), chronon = cal_symmetry454$week(1L), cycle = cal_symmetry454$month(1L)) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] W2  # Day of week (inherited from cal_isoweek) day_of_week(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sat # Day of year (inherited from cal_time_civil_midnight) day_of_year(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] D363 date(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955-11-13 yearweek(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955 W52 yearmonth(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] M-170

title: Extending mixtime
text: default formatting time vectors defined chronon_format_linear() linear time chronon_format_cyclical() cyclical time. methods return mixtime format strings used format print time vectors. mixtime format string glue style string time granules wrapped {} replaced granule labels corresponding time points. two types granule labels useful time format strings: lin(<time unit>) - Granule labels linear time units, usually largest granule (e.g. lin(year(1L))). labels produced linear_labels(). cyc(<time unit>, <cycle unit>) - Granule labels cyclical time units, labels relative cycle (e.g. cyc(month(1L), year(1L)) month within year). labels produced cyclical_labels(). <time unit> <cycle unit> placeholders format string must evaluate time unit calendar. convenience, unevaluated named time units (e.g. lin(year)) default size 1 time unit (e.g. lin(year(1L))), calendar inferred time vector formatted. also possible explicitly specify calendar (e.g. lin(cal_symmetry454$year(1L))), allows time formats use granules different calendars.

title: Extending mixtime
text: Linear time formats dispatch chronon calendar. calendar needed disambiguate identical time units exist multiple calendars. case, cal_symmetry454$day common civil calendars, cal_symmetry454$week shares implementation cal_isoweek$week - cases Symmetry 454 format method needed display dates appropriately. default time formatting strings place, linear time vectors now informative default labels:
code: # Simply display years as numbers (e.g. "1970", "1971", ...) S7::method(   chronon_format_linear,   list(cal_symmetry454$year, S7::class_any) ) <- function(x, cal) "{lin(year(1L))}"  # Display labelled months as month within year (e.g. "1970 Jan", "1970 Feb", ...) S7::method(   chronon_format_linear,   list(cal_symmetry454$month, S7::class_any) ) <- function(x, cal) "{lin(year(1L))}-{cyc(month(1L), year(1L), label=TRUE, abbreviate=TRUE)}"  # Display weeks as week in month in year (e.g. "1970-01-W1", "1970-01-W2", ...) S7::method(   chronon_format_linear,   list(cal_symmetry454$week, S7::new_S3_class("cal_symmetry454")) ) <- function(x, cal) "{lin(year(1L))}-{cyc(month(1L), year(1L), label=TRUE, abbreviate=TRUE)}-W{cyc(week(1L), month(1L))}"  # Format days as day in week in month in year (e.g. "1970-Jan-W1-Mon", "1970-Jan-W1-Tue", ...) S7::method(   chronon_format_linear,   list(cal_symmetry454$day, S7::new_S3_class("cal_symmetry454")) ) <- function(x, cal) "{lin(year(1L))}-{cyc(month(1L), year(1L), label=TRUE, abbreviate=TRUE)}-W{cyc(week(1L), month(1L))}-{cyc(day(1L), week(1L), label=TRUE, abbreviate=TRUE)}" # Years are formatted as YYYY year(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955  # Months are formatted as YYYY Mon yearmonth(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955-Nov  # Weeks are formatted as YYYY-MM-WW yearweek(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 1955-Nov-W2  # Days are formatted as YYYY-MM-WW-DD linear_time(as.Date(c("1985-10-26", "1955-11-05", "1955-11-12")), chronon = cal_symmetry454$day(1L)) #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 1985-Oct-W4-Sat 1955-Nov-W1-Sat 1955-Nov-W2-Sat

Defining methods cyclical time formats necessary particular formatting labels desired (e.g. month year day week labels). combinations chronon cycle fall back default format, combines time unit abbreviation cycle label (e.g. “W1” week 1 month year). methods used formatting cyclical time vectors, month year:

S7::method(   chronon_format_cyclical,   list(cal_symmetry454$month, cal_symmetry454$year) ) <- function(x, y) "{cyc(month,year,label=TRUE,abbreviate=TRUE)}" month_of_year(as.Date("1955-11-12"), calendar = cal_symmetry454) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Nov

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: mixtime provides flexible time classes time series analysis forecasting mixed temporal granularities. designed handling temporal data different frequencies, making ideal : Changing observation frequencies (e.g., historical quarterly records now reported monthly) Analysis involving data different temporal resolutions (e.g., quarterly tourism monthly turnover) Forecasting temporal reconciliation across multiple time scales

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: 📈 Linear Time - Create linear time vectors linear_time() helpers: yearquarter(), yearmonth(), yearweek(), date() 🔄 Cyclical Time - Create cyclical time vectors cyclical_time() helpers: month_of_year(), day_of_year(), day_of_month(), time_of_day(), day_of_week(), week_of_year() 🕰️ Time types Discrete continuous time models Timezone support chronons Custom granule sizes (e.g. week(2L) fortnights) Mixed granularity vectors different temporal resolution time (series) 📅 Calendar Systems - Support several calendars: cal_gregorian - Gregorian dates (e.g. 2026-02-17) cal_isoweek - ISO week dates (e.g. 2026-W8-Tue) calendars coming, including custom censored calendars 🧮 Temporal Operations Rounding: floor_time(), round_time(), ceiling_time() Sequencing: seq() linear cyclical time points

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations

code: # The Gregorian calendar cal_gregorian #> <cal_gregorian> #> Time units: #>   - year #>   - quarter #>   - month #>   - day #>   - ampm #>   - hour #>   - minute #>   - second #>   - millisecond  # A 1-month time unit cal_gregorian$month(1L) # (1L is integer 1) #> <mixtime::tu_month> int 1 #>  @ tz: chr ""  # A 2-week time unit (fortnights) cal_isoweek$week(2L) #> <mixtime::tu_week> int 2 #>  @ tz: chr ""

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: linear time vector uses time units define resolution time points, known chronon (smallest unit time). input time different resolution chronon, automatically converted. Discrete time models (integer-based values) used default, however continuous time models (double-based values) can used discrete = FALSE allow fractional chronons. Linear time helper functions available quickly create common time points. mixtime package allows time different granulities combined single vector.
code: linear_time(demo_date, chronon = cal_gregorian$month(1L)) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026 Feb # February 22nd is 75% through the month (in non-leap years) linear_time(demo_date, chronon = cal_gregorian$month(1L), discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026 Feb 75.0% # Create time vectors at different granularities yearquarter(demo_date) + 0:7 #> <mixtime[8]> #> [1] 2026 Q1 2026 Q2 2026 Q3 2026 Q4 2027 Q1 2027 Q2 2027 Q3 2027 Q4 yearmonth(demo_date) + 0:11 #> <mixtime[12]> #>  [1] 2026 Feb 2026 Mar 2026 Apr 2026 May 2026 Jun 2026 Jul 2026 Aug 2026 Sep #>  [9] 2026 Oct 2026 Nov 2026 Dec 2027 Jan yearweek(demo_date) + 0:10 #> <mixtime[11]> #>  [1] 2026 W08 2026 W09 2026 W10 2026 W11 2026 W12 2026 W13 2026 W14 2026 W15 #>  [9] 2026 W16 2026 W17 2026 W18 date(demo_date) + 0:6 #> <mixtime[7]> #> [1] 2026-02-22 2026-02-23 2026-02-24 2026-02-25 2026-02-26 2026-02-27 2026-02-28 c(   year(demo_date), yearquarter(demo_date),    yearmonth(demo_date), yearweek(demo_date) ) #> <mixtime[4]> #> [1] 2026     2026 Q1  2026 Feb 2026 W08

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: cyclical time vector defined two calendar time units: chronon (smaller unit time) cycle (larger unit chronon loops ). several cyclical time helper functions convenience.
code: # The `calendar` argument provides a masking scope to `chronon` and `cycle` cyclical_time(demo_date, chronon = day(1L), cycle = week(1L), calendar = cal_isoweek) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sun # Extract cyclical components month_of_year(demo_date) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Feb week_of_year(demo_date) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] W08 day_of_week(demo_date) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sun  # Continuous cyclical time shows progress through chronons day_of_week(demo_time, discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sun 77.1%

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: linear cyclical time vectors support timezones via tz argument.
code: demo_time #> [1] "2026-02-22 18:30:42 UTC" # Same day (Sunday) in LA date(demo_time, tz = "America/Los_Angeles") #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-02-22 PST date(demo_time, tz = "America/Los_Angeles", discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-02-22 PST 43.8% day_of_week(demo_time, tz = "America/Los_Angeles") #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Sun PST  # Next day (Monday) in Melbourne (23% through the 23rd) date(demo_time, tz = "Australia/Melbourne") #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-02-23 AEDT date(demo_time, tz = "Australia/Melbourne", discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-02-23 AEDT 23.0% day_of_week(demo_time, tz = "Australia/Melbourne") #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] Mon AEDT

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations
text: Linear time points can adjusted floor, ceiling, rounded specified time unit.
code: # Round dates to different granularities floor_time(demo_date, cal_gregorian$month(1L)) #> Date of length 0 round_time(demo_date, cal_isoweek$week(1L)) #> Date of length 0 ceiling_time(demo_date, cal_gregorian$month(1L)) #> Date of length 0

title: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations

code: # Integer increments (advances by chronon's natural unit) seq(yearmonth(demo_date), by = 1L, length.out = 10) #> <mixtime[10]> #>  [1] 2026 Feb 2026 Mar 2026 Apr 2026 May 2026 Jun 2026 Jul 2026 Aug 2026 Sep #>  [9] 2026 Oct 2026 Nov  # Calendar time units allow sequencing by other units seq(date(demo_date), by = cal_gregorian$month(1L), length.out = 8) #> <mixtime[8]> #> [1] 2026-02-22 2026-03-22 2026-04-22 2026-05-22 2026-06-22 2026-07-22 2026-08-22 #> [8] 2026-09-22

text: Symmetry454 calendar (Sym454) perennial solar calendar proposed Dr. Irv Bromberg. preserves traditional 12-month structure 7-day week, months arranged symmetrical 4-5-4 week pattern per quarter. Every month starts Monday whole number weeks, meaning month ever contains partial week. following time units available Symmetry454 calendar (cal_sym454$). year(): Year unit month(): Month unit week(): Week unit day(): Day unit hour(): Hour unit minute(): Minute unit second(): Second unit millisecond(): Millisecond unit

title: Symmetry454 time unit classes — calendar_sym454
text: Rather intercalary days, Symmetry454 uses leap week appended December every 5 6 years, making December 5-week month leap years. year leap year (52 * year + 146) %% 293 < 52. yields mean year 365 + 71/293 days (≈ 365 days, 5 hours, 48 minutes, 56.5 seconds), intentionally slightly shorter mean northward equinoctial year.

title: Cyclical time points — cyclical_time
text: data Input data convert cyclical time. Can : Numeric values (interpreted chronons, 1-indexed) Character strings (parsed dates/times) Date POSIXct objects time objects chronon time unit representing chronon (smallest indivisible time unit), evaluated context calendar. Use unquoted expressions like day(1L) month(1L). Chronons specific calendar can also used (e.g. cal_isoweek$day(1L)). cycle time unit representing cycle (larger time unit defines period), evaluated context calendar. Use unquoted expressions like week(1L) year(1L). time units ordered coarsest (e.g. year) finest (e.g second). discrete Logical. TRUE (default), returns integer positions within cycle (discrete time model). FALSE, returns fractional positions allowing representation partial time units (continuous time model). calendar Calendar system used evaluate chronon cycle. Defaults time_calendar(data) existing time objects. Common options include cal_gregorian cal_isoweek.

what: mixtime: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations — mixtime-package
title: mixtime: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations — mixtime-package
text: Flexible time classes time series analysis forecasting mixed temporal granularities. Supports linear cyclical time representations discrete continuous forms, timezone support, across multiple calendar systems including Gregorian ISO week date calendars. Time points stored numerically relative chronon; atomic time unit defined calendar. Calendrical arithmetic enables conversion time units (e.g. days months) calendar systems. Multi-unit arithmetic allows temporal analysis granules common calendars (e.g. fortnights 2-week units). Time vectors different granularities (e.g. monthly quarterly) can combined single vector, making mixtime ideal data changes observation frequency time requires temporal reconciliation across scales. package extensible, allowing users define custom calendars build upon civil astronomical time systems.

title: mixtime: Mixed Temporal Vectors and Operations — mixtime-package

title: Create a mixtime vector — mixtime
code: # Create a mixtime for today mixtime(Sys.Date()) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-04-08  # Create a mixtime for the current date and time mixtime(Sys.time()) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-04-08 00:47:00  # Convert time from tsibble units to mixtime mixtime(tsibble::yearmonth("2024 Jan")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2024 Jan  # Create a mixtime for the time of day (cyclical time) mixtime(Sys.time(), cycle = cal_gregorian$day(1L)) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 00:47:00  # Specify a timezone for the chronon mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$second(1L, tz = Sys.timezone())) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-04-08 00:47:00 mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$second(1L, tz = "Pacific/Honolulu")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-04-07 14:47:00 HST mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$second(1L, tz = "Australia/Melbourne")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-04-08 10:47:00 AEST  # Dates (and all granularities) can have timezones mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$day(1L, tz = Sys.timezone())) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-04-08 mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$day(1L, tz = "Pacific/Honolulu")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-04-07 HST mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$day(1L, tz = "Australia/Melbourne")) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-04-08 AEST  # Continuous time tracks progress within the chronon mixtime(Sys.time(), chronon = cal_gregorian$day(1L, tz = Sys.timezone()), discrete = FALSE) #> <mixtime[1]> #> [1] 2026-04-08 3.3%  # Mixtime can combine different granularities and timezones in a vector now <- Sys.time() c(   # Datetime (second chronon) in UTC   mixtime(now),   # Date (minute chronon) in local timezone   mixtime(now, chronon = cal_gregorian$minute(1L, tz = Sys.timezone())),   # Month (month chronon) in UTC   mixtime(now, chronon = cal_gregorian$month(1L)) ) #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 2026-04-08 00:47:01 2026-04-08 00:47    2026 Apr

code: # Linear time sequences with integer by seq(yearmonth("2020-01-01"), yearmonth("2020-12-01")) #> <mixtime[12]> #>  [1] 2020 Jan 2020 Feb 2020 Mar 2020 Apr 2020 May 2020 Jun 2020 Jul 2020 Aug #>  [9] 2020 Sep 2020 Oct 2020 Nov 2020 Dec seq(yearquarter("2020-01-01"), length.out = 5, by = 3) #> <mixtime[5]> #> [1] 2020 Q1 2020 Q4 2021 Q3 2022 Q2 2023 Q1  # Linear time sequences with string intervals seq(date("2020-01-01"), date("2020-12-31"), by = "1 month") #> <mixtime[12]> #>  [1] 2020-01-01 2020-02-01 2020-03-01 2020-04-01 2020-05-01 2020-06-01 #>  [7] 2020-07-01 2020-08-01 2020-09-01 2020-10-01 2020-11-01 2020-12-01 seq(yearmonth("2020-01-01"), yearmonth("2025-01-01"), by = "1 year") #> <mixtime[6]> #> [1] 2020 Jan 2021 Jan 2022 Jan 2023 Jan 2024 Jan 2025 Jan seq(date("2020-01-01"), length.out = 10, by = "2 weeks") #> <mixtime[10]> #>  [1] 2020-01-01 2020-01-15 2020-01-29 2020-02-12 2020-02-26 2020-03-11 #>  [7] 2020-03-25 2020-04-08 2020-04-22 2020-05-06  # Linear time sequences with time units seq(yearmonth("2020-01-01"), yearmonth("2020-12-01"), by = cal_gregorian$month(2L)) #> <mixtime[6]> #> [1] 2020 Jan 2020 Mar 2020 May 2020 Jul 2020 Sep 2020 Nov seq(date("2020-01-01"), length.out = 5, by = cal_gregorian$year(1L)) #> <mixtime[5]> #> [1] 2020-01-01 2021-01-01 2022-01-01 2023-01-01 2024-01-01 seq(date("2020-01-01"), date("2020-01-31"), by = cal_gregorian$day(7L)) #> <mixtime[5]> #> [1] 2020-01-01 2020-01-08 2020-01-15 2020-01-22 2020-01-29  # Handling invalid dates with on_invalid seq(date("2020-01-31"), length.out = 3, by = "1 month")  # warns, uses nearest #> Warning: The cycle offset (31 days) has produced time points that overflow the month #> cycle. #> ! Using the nearest valid time points in the cycle, `on_invalid = "nearest"` #>   (the default). #> ℹ Specify `on_invalid` explicitly to suppress this warning. #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 2020-01-31 2020-02-29 2020-03-31 seq(date("2020-01-31"), length.out = 3, by = "1 month", on_invalid = "nearest") #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 2020-01-31 2020-02-29 2020-03-31 seq(date("2020-01-31"), length.out = 3, by = "1 month", on_invalid = "overflow") #> <mixtime[3]> #> [1] 2020-01-31 2020-03-02 2020-03-31  # Cyclical time sequences seq(month_of_year(0L), month_of_year(11L)) #> <mixtime[12]> #>  [1] Dec Jan Feb Mar Apr May Jun Jul Aug Sep Oct Nov # seq(month_of_year(5L), month_of_year(3L), by = cal_gregorian$month(2L)) seq(day_of_week(0L), day_of_week(6L), by = 1) #> <mixtime[7]> #> [1] Wed Thu Fri Sat Sun Mon Tue
